learning_outcomes.description: Analyse and evaluate the transformations facing the publishing industry in the age of AI, demonstrating the ability to apply knowledge in a global and interdisciplinary context.
Critically appraise the evolving role of writers and editors in relation to emerging technologies, applying ethical reasoning and professional judgement in response to industry change.

Investigate the ethical, creative, and professional challenges of authorship and editorial practice in the age of AI, demonstrating awareness of diverse perspectives, integrity, and social responsibility.

Exercise editorial judgement in response to texts and communicate editorial decisions effectively to authors, demonstrating critical thinking and sensitivity to managing relationships with diverse stakeholders.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Practical/ Demonstration, Presentation, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Through completion of a series of in-class tasks and activities, this assessment will enhance your understanding of the challenges facing authors and editors and those operating in the publishing industry in the post-digital/AI era. You will also demonstrate your understanding of course materials through ongoing conversations with your peers and tutors.

Activities to be completed – outlined on LearnUQ – range from in-class writing exercises and group discussion to attendance at local, regional or national writing/editing/publishing events. These activities will facilitate reflection on issues of inequality, cultural difference, and intersectionality. Consideration will be given to editorial practice and intercultural, ethical and relational communication. Topical industry issues and points of contention highlighted by and emerging from activities, student experience and events attended will also be discussed in class.

In-class activities, based on seminar material; completed in seminar; duration 15-20minutes.

From Week 4 to Week 12 in-class writing and group discussion tasks and activities will take place during each weekly seminar. During the seminar, you will be given 15-20 minutes to write a short response to stimulus, to respond to questions regarding a set reading, and discuss key concepts and industry-related issues raised in the seminar materials.

There are 8 opportunities across the semester, each worth 6%. Only your best 5 results count toward your mark (/30%) for the assessment. While there is flexibility to miss up to 3 sessions — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required, we would encourage you to attend as many seminars as possible.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual exercise, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below. As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available to complete each exercise at the scheduled date, time and venue.

The writing activities are handwritten, closed-book tasks: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag or under your desk, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise. You may bring suitable writing implements (in a clear container), water in a clear bottle with the label removed, and disposable earplugs.

The discussion activities will be delivered during seminar and are also closed-book tasks: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag or under your desk, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise.

You will share 'workings' and reflections in class as you undertake written activities. Your mark will reflect a ‘meaningful contribution’ to class discussion. Your contribution is 'meaningful' where it adds to, augments, the co-creation of knowledge and understanding. This may include (but is not limited to): asking thoughtful and relevant questions; sharing appropriate and relevant responses or commentary; engaging in peer feedback; or assisting peers in their completion of assigned activities.

While your best five tutorials will make up your final mark, we encourage you to participate in as many tutorials as you are able.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Line-edit a selected sample manuscript (10 points) and write a responsive Editorial Letter to its 'author' (20 points). The Line-edit should reflect understanding and application of basic editorial functions and practices. The accompanying Editorial Letter should provide a concise critique of the manuscript, which includes detailed feedback, structural consideration, and copy level discussion. The Editorial Letter must include at least two recommendations for improving aspects of the work. You might note, for example: voice, plot, character development, pacing, and dialogue. You could also discuss language level concerns and offer careful thought on the broader strengths and weaknesses of the sample manuscript. Together, your Line-edit and Editorial Letter should reflect understanding and working knowledge of industry standards and expectations.

To be submitted: Line-edited MS sample and Editorial Letter (800 words)
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Write an academic essay that critically assesses an aspect of the evolving role of authors/editors and the impacts of current and emerging technologies in this post-digital age of AI. Focus on one or more of the many social, historical, economic, political, or cultural issues, challenges and changes affecting the modern author/editor and writing/editing as a practice. You should discuss the significance of the issue, challenge or change, and what it means for writing/editorial practices. Use citations with MLA style, include a reference list with at least 8 items.

To be submitted: Essay (1500 words (excluding refs))
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
